name: Red Summer 1919
description: The Red Summer of 1919 refers to the period from late spring through early autumn of that year during which white supremacist terrorism and racial riots took place in more than three dozen cities across the United States, as well as in a rural county in Arkansas. While the term primarily describes the violence inflicted upon African Americans, it also encompasses broader societal tensions following World War I. Proponents of alternative historical interpretations sometimes suggest the Red Summer was a deliberate, coordinated effort to suppress Black advancement and maintain white supremacy, potentially fueled by secret societies or government agendas. Evidence cited often includes the scale and geographic distribution of the violence, the perceived inadequacy of official responses, and the historical context of Jim Crow laws and racial discrimination. Mainstream historical analysis attributes the Red Summer to a complex interplay of factors: the Great Migration of African Americans from the rural South to northern industrial cities, competition for jobs and housing, the return of white veterans seeking employment, and the pervasive racism of the era. Scholars emphasize the role of local conditions, economic anxieties, and pre-existing racial tensions in triggering the violence. While some conspiracy theories suggest a centralized orchestration, academic historians generally view the events as interconnected but largely spontaneous outbreaks of racial violence driven by local circumstances and the broader social climate. The legacy of the Red Summer is a stark reminder of the deep-seated racial inequalities in American history and continues to inform discussions about race relations and social justice.
subtitle: A Season of Racial Violence and Upheaval

claims.claim: The Red Summer was a coordinated effort to suppress Black advancement.
claims.evidence: Scale and geographic distribution of violence; perceived inadequacy of official responses; historical context of Jim Crow laws.
claims.criticism: Mainstream historians attribute the violence to a complex interplay of local factors: economic anxieties, racial tensions, and the aftermath of World War I, rather than a centralized conspiracy.

claims.claim: The government deliberately failed to protect African Americans during the riots.
claims.evidence: Lack of effective intervention by law enforcement; instances of police complicity in the violence; biased media coverage.
claims.criticism: While acknowledging the failures of law enforcement and systemic racism, historians point to the limitations of government power and the deeply ingrained prejudices of the time as contributing factors, rather than a deliberate government policy of inaction.

claims.claim: Secret societies played a role in instigating and perpetuating the violence.
claims.evidence: Alleged involvement of Ku Klux Klan members and other white supremacist groups; rumors of clandestine meetings and planning sessions.
claims.criticism: While the KKK and other white supremacist groups undoubtedly contributed to the violence, there is limited evidence of a coordinated, nationwide conspiracy orchestrated by secret societies. Mainstream historical accounts focus on the role of local chapters and individual actors.

keyFigures.name: Ida B. Wells
keyFigures.role: Documented and protested racial violence; advocated for civil rights.

keyFigures.name: W.E.B. Du Bois
keyFigures.role: Advocated for Black self-defense and challenged racial inequality.

keyFigures.name: David Levering Lewis
keyFigures.role: Historian; author of 'When Harlem Was in Vogue', providing historical context.

mythologyConnections: The concept of a 'fiery trial' or 'cleansing fire' can be seen as a distorted parallel to the violence of the Red Summer.
The struggle for racial equality echoes mythological themes of overcoming oppression and achieving liberation.

timeline.event: Increased racial tensions due to the Great Migration and World War I.
Outbreak of racial violence in several cities, including Elaine, Arkansas and Washington, D.C.
Escalation of racial riots and lynchings across the United States.
Decline in the frequency of major racial incidents, but continued racial tensions and discrimination.

sources.author: David F. Krugler
sources.work: 1919, The Year of Racial Violence: How African Americans Fought Back
sources.title: David F. Krugler

sources.title: Cameron McWhirter
